title: Question: Using Verilator with Buck
body:
---


Author Name: **Arjen Roodselaar**
Original Redmine Message: 2564 from https://www.veripool.org



---

I have been working on a project that has both a sizable amount of C++ as well as SystemVerilog. I use Verilator to write unit tests for modules (using Gtest) as well as several larger integration tests where input stimuli are generated using a C++ library. To drive the compilation process I have been using the Buck build system (buckbuild.com). This has worked well so far, but I have had to hack around some rough edges in Verilator and I need to properly address these. I am willing to contribute patches, but before doing so I'd like to solicit discussion and make sure they would be accepted upstream. Apologies in advance for the wall of text.

First off a few words about Buck; the philosophy behind Buck is to group source files into libraries, which in turn are composed to form programs. Think C/C++ files grouped into libraries, which are combined, usually with one or more source files that contain a main() function into a binary. Buck wants all inputs to these build targets to be declared in advance, allowing it to compute a DAG and determine which parts of a project need to be rebuilt when files change, allow fine-grained caching of build artifacts, mixing of static and dynamic libraries as to reduce build times, etc.

In line with this philosophy I would like to group Verilog modules into libraries and compose larger designs by declaring dependencies between these libraries. I would like to do this by running Verilator as a preprocessor on a number of Verilog module files and saving the output as a "library file". This would somewhat mimic the behavior of other Verilog tools and this would allow me to "compile" different flavors of the same library; one for Quartus, one for Verilator, possibly one for ModelSim etc. Once these libraries have been generated I would like to be able to pick any one of the modules within that library and treat it as a top level module so I can then generate the C++ files for the model and use it in my unit tests. When considering the Verilog module dependency tree however, it may happen that some modules in a library instantiate modules in the same library. Nothing too crazy in this hierarchy, definitely no loops, but the library may not be entirely flat either. Verilog does not seem to like this (Specified --top-module 'X' isn't at the top level, it's under another cell 'Y').

1) Is there a specific reason for Verilator to treat this as an error? Would it be ok to add the ability to simply ignore these modules, or maybe even change the default behavior?

Due to the fact that Buck wants all dependencies declared and some limitations in how much information can be carried between dependencies defining build targets, it would be really helpful if Verilator can be invoked without an explicit source file, but instead be given one or more libraries using the -v or -y flags and a top module for which to compile code. Combined with the change above, this would picking arbitrary top modules out of a collection of libraries, as long as Verilator sees all required definitions for the chosen sub-tree.

2) Is there a reason for Verilator always requiring the path of the file containing the definition of the top module to be an argument? Or would this be up for change in a reasonable way?

Verilator seems to optimize for equal sized C++ compilation units. As a result it may generate source and header files which may not be known in advance. This is fine when using Make, but for Buck this is a non-starter. As a workaround I have written a wrapper which concatenates all source and header files generated by Verilator together, but this is fragile.

3) Would it be acceptable to add a mode where Verilator generates source and header files with names that are strictly predictable given its input flags? This could result in significantly sized compilation units, but I am willing to address that problem once build times become unacceptable.

Thanks in advance!
